name: NavNestedGraphs
tagline: Model a self-contained onboarding sub-flow on Nav3's flat back stack and pop the whole graph as one unit.
whatItDoes: The app opens INSIDE a 3-step onboarding wizard: a Welcome screen with 'Next', a 'Pick a favorite planet' screen where you tap one planet to highlight it and then 'Continue', and a 'You're all set!' screen with 'Get started'. Back inside onboarding walks you between those three steps. Tapping 'Get started' finishes onboarding and drops you into the main app at a Categories list (Rocky Planets / Gas Giants), where you drill into a category's planets and then into a single planet's detail. Crucially, once you've finished onboarding, Back from the Categories screen exits the app and never walks you back into the (now finished) wizard.
mentalModel: Nav3 has no first-class nested-graph type, so you model a sub-flow by grouping its keys into a Set and 'completing' it with back-stack surgery: 'removeAll { it in onboardingKeys }' then 'add(CategoriesKey)' pops the entire graph as one unit.

screens.name: WelcomeScreen
screens.role: Graph A step 1: greets the user and offers a single 'Next' button to advance within onboarding.

screens.name: ChooseFavoriteScreen
screens.role: Graph A step 2: a tappable list of all planets where you select one favorite (local UI state) before 'Continue' becomes enabled.

screens.name: DoneScreen
screens.role: Graph A step 3: confirms onboarding is done and offers 'Get started', the hand-off that pops the whole sub-flow.

screens.name: CategoriesScreen
screens.role: Graph B step 1 (main app start): a list of categories; tapping one opens its items.

screens.name: ItemsScreen
screens.role: Graph B step 2: a header for the chosen category plus a scrolling list of its planets; tapping one opens detail.

screens.name: DetailScreen
screens.role: Graph B step 3: shows one planet's title and blurb resolved from the id in its DetailKey.

concepts.plain: A self-contained multi-step flow (here onboarding) that runs as one cohesive unit and then hands off to the main app. The defining trait is that completing it pops the ENTIRE flow off the stack, not one screen at a time.

concepts.term: Modeling a graph as a key Set
concepts.plain: Since Nav3's back stack is a flat list, the 'graph membership' is just 'onboardingKeys = setOf(WelcomeKey, ChooseFavoriteKey, DoneKey)'. That Set IS the definition of which destinations belong to the onboarding nested graph.

Completing the sub-flow is done by directly editing the list: 'backStack.removeAll { it in onboardingKeys }' deletes every onboarding key, then 'backStack.add(CategoriesKey)' enters the main app. This is the Nav3 stand-in for Nav2's 'popUpTo(graph) { inclusive = true }'.

concepts.term: NavKey + data object vs data class
concepts.plain: Every destination implements NavKey. Argument-free steps (WelcomeKey, CategoriesKey) are singleton 'data object' keys; steps that carry data (ItemsKey(categoryId), DetailKey(itemId)) are 'data class' keys that bundle the destination AND its arguments together.
'val backStack = rememberNavBackStack(WelcomeKey)' creates and remembers the stack seeded with a Welcome key, so the app deliberately STARTS inside the onboarding sub-flow rather than the main app.

The 'entryProvider { entry<KeyType> { ... } }' DSL maps each key type to a screen. Here the entry blocks are split into two commented sections, Graph A and Graph B, so the 'two graphs sharing one flat stack' idea is visible in code.

NavDisplay's 'onBack = { backStack.removeLastOrNull() }' just removes the top key. Inside onboarding it walks Done -> Choose -> Welcome; in the main app it walks Detail -> Items -> Categories; because finishOnboarding already deleted Graph A, Back can never cross from Graph B into Graph A.

Screens are 'dumb' composables that only render and report taps via callbacks; nav keys carry only an id (e.g. DetailKey(itemId)) and the screen resolves the full object via 'itemById(id)', keeping each key tiny and serializable.

files.role: The entire app: sample planet data, navigation keys grouped into Graph A (onboarding) and Graph B (main), the MainActivity, AppNavigation with the finishOnboarding back-stack surgery and grouped entryProvider, all six screen composables, and previews.

highlights.title: Keys grouped into two graphs, with the onboarding Set
highlights.explain: Graph A's three steps are argument-free 'data object' keys (WelcomeKey, ChooseFavoriteKey, DoneKey); Graph B mixes a 'data object' CategoriesKey with 'data class' keys ItemsKey(categoryId) and DetailKey(itemId) that carry arguments. The final line, 'onboardingKeys = setOf(WelcomeKey, ChooseFavoriteKey, DoneKey)', is the lightweight definition of 'which destinations are in the nested graph' that finishOnboarding later relies on. This grouping is the whole nested-graph model, since Nav3 has no graph object.

highlights.title: Seeding the back stack inside the onboarding sub-flow
highlights.explain: 'rememberNavBackStack(WelcomeKey)' creates and remembers the back stack seeded with WelcomeKey, so the app deliberately opens INSIDE Graph A (onboarding) rather than the main app. Because NavBackStack is a MutableList<NavKey>, the code gets add(), removeLastOrNull(), and removeAll{} for free, and those list operations are literally how navigation happens in Nav3.

highlights.title: finishOnboarding: pop the whole sub-flow as a unit
highlights.explain: This is the single most important function in the lesson. 'backStack.removeAll { it in onboardingKeys }' deletes EVERY onboarding key at once, then 'backStack.add(CategoriesKey)' enters Graph B at its start. After it runs the stack is exactly [CategoriesKey], so Back exits the app and can never return into onboarding. It is the hand-written equivalent of Nav2's 'popUpTo(onboarding_graph) { inclusive = true }'.

highlights.title: NavDisplay with a flat onBack and a grouped entryProvider
highlights.explain: NavDisplay renders whatever key is on top of backStack. 'onBack = { backStack.removeLastOrNull() }' is a single flat pop that serves BOTH graphs' internal Back, and removeLastOrNull is a safe no-op on an empty stack. The entryProvider DSL that follows is split into two labelled sections so that two graphs sharing one flat stack stays visible in the code.

highlights.title: Graph A entries: 'Get started' wires to finishOnboarding
highlights.explain: Each onboarding step's entry<> block pushes the NEXT step ('add(ChooseFavoriteKey)', 'add(DoneKey)') to walk forward within Graph A, and its in-flow Back pops one step. The key teaching moment is DoneScreen's 'onGetStarted = finishOnboarding': the only way to LEAVE the sub-flow is the unit pop, never a one-screen-at-a-time slide into Graph B.

highlights.title: Graph B entries: id-carrying keys resolved to objects
highlights.explain: The drill-down pushes 'ItemsKey(categoryId)' and 'DetailKey(itemId)', then each entry block reads the id off the key ('key.categoryId', 'key.itemId') and resolves the full object via 'categoryById' / 'itemsInCategory' / 'itemById'. Passing only an id keeps every key tiny and serializable, and because Graph A was already popped, the first Back here exits the app instead of re-entering onboarding.

newVsPrev: NavFourScreen taught a single flat back stack of four screens with simple forward-push / pop navigation. NavNestedGraphs keeps that flat Nav3 back stack but layers a NESTED-GRAPH model on top: it groups keys into two graphs (an onboarding sub-flow A and a main app B), seeds the stack inside Graph A, and adds the finishOnboarding back-stack surgery ('removeAll { it in onboardingKeys }' + 'add(CategoriesKey)') that pops the ENTIRE sub-flow as one unit so Back never crosses back into onboarding. It also splits the entryProvider into two clearly-commented graph sections.
tryThis: In finishOnboarding, comment out 'backStack.removeAll { it in onboardingKeys }' so it only does 'backStack.add(CategoriesKey)'. Run it, finish onboarding, then press Back from Categories: you'll now walk back into DoneScreen, proving why the unit pop matters.

Add a fourth onboarding step: create '@Serializable data object SetupKey : NavKey', add it to the onboardingKeys Set, give it an entry<SetupKey> block, and push it from WelcomeScreen's onNext. Confirm finishOnboarding still pops the whole flow without changing finishOnboarding itself.

Change 'rememberNavBackStack(WelcomeKey)' to 'rememberNavBackStack(CategoriesKey)' and relaunch: the app now starts in Graph B and skips onboarding entirely, showing the seed key chooses which graph you start in.
Filter Logcat by the tag 'NEST' and watch the messages from AppNavigation and finishOnboarding fire as you walk through and complete the sub-flow.
In ChooseFavoriteScreen, store the selectedId somewhere visible on DoneScreen (e.g. pass it forward) to see why the favorite is intentionally NOT part of the nav key.
gotchas: There is NO real graph object. 'Graph A' and 'Graph B' exist only as commented groupings of keys plus the 'onboardingKeys' Set; the actual back stack is one flat MutableList<NavKey>. If you add a new onboarding key but forget to put it in onboardingKeys, finishOnboarding will leave it stranded on the stack.

The favorite-planet selection is LOCAL UI state ('var selectedId by remember { mutableStateOf<Int?>(null) }'), not navigation and not saved across process death. It is just a highlight to make the step feel real and is discarded when you leave the screen.
Lookups use 'first { }' (categoryById, itemById), which THROWS if no id matches. That's safe only because every key is built from a known id; passing a bad id would crash.

The app intentionally starts in onboarding every launch because the stack is always seeded with WelcomeKey. There is no persisted 'has the user already onboarded?' flag, so a real app would need one to skip the sub-flow on later launches.
runTest: Open the project in Android Studio and run it on an emulator or device (or 'cd NavNestedGraphs && ./gradlew installDebug' then launch the app). You start on the Welcome screen; tap Next, tap a planet then Continue, then tap 'Get started' to finish onboarding and land on the Categories list. The thing to verify: press the system Back button from Categories - the app should EXIT, never returning into the onboarding wizard. Also confirm Back walks correctly within each graph (Done -> Choose -> Welcome before finishing; Detail -> Items -> Categories after). Filter Logcat by 'NEST' to see the navigation breadcrumbs.
